coredata.dc:title: Optimization of graphene quantum dots by chemical exfoliation from graphite powders and carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Siyong
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Chiang, Yu-Ming
Tzou, Dong-Ying
Chen, Yu-Fu
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
coredata.dc:description: This work adopts one- and two-step modified Hummers' method to chemically exfoliate graphene nanosheets (GNs) from graphite flake powders and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). The protocol for making the GN products involves one- and two-step exfoliation using KMnO4 oxidation agent. Experimental results reveal that the two-step exfoliation induce high efficiency of chemical oxidation, especially for the synthesis of GNs from CNT precursor. The thermal reduction enables the removal of surface functionalities, thus narrowing the band gap of GN-based electrodes, e.g., from ∼2.12 eV to ∼1.60 eV. The two series of graphene samples, prepared from graphite flake and CNTs, are chemically cut into nanoscale and serve as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The photoluminescence (PL) excitation behavior is strongly affected by oxidation level and band gap of GQD products. After the thermal reduction, the PL peak shifts to lower wavelength region (i.e., 427–451 nm), offering a blue-shift behavior. The blue-shift behavior primarily originated from surface functionalization, particle size and shape of GQDs. Without any thermal reduction, the GQD suspensions exhibit an improved PL intensity, attributed to fast carrier‒carrier scattering dominates over the electron‒phonon scattering onto the basal plane of graphene sheets due to their high oxidation level and large band gap. As a result, this study offers an efficient way to tune the band gap of GQDs, favoring the development of optoelectronic and biological applications using graphene-based materials in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical exfoliation
Graphene sheets
Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Modified hummers' method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene by the modified Hummers' method
Preparation and PL property of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for describing the formation of graphene oxide from graphite flake through one- and two-step chemical exfoliation: (i)-(ii)-(iii)-(iv) (one step) and (i)-(ii)-(ii’)-(iii)-(iv) (two-step).
Schematic diagram for describing the formation of graphene oxide from CNTs through one- and two-step chemical exfoliation: (i)-(ii)-(iii)-(iv) (one step) and (i)-(ii)-(ii’)-(iii)-(iv) (two-step).

(a) FE-SEM image of pristine graphite flask and HR-TEM micrographs for the graphite after (b) 1st and (c) 2nd chemical exfoliation. (d) FE-SEM image of pristine CNTs and HR-TEM micrographs for the CNTs after (e) 1st and (f) 2nd chemical exfoliation.

Typical XRD patterns of different carbon samples: (a) G and (b) CNT series. The d 002 interlayer distance and ID/IG ratio as a function of carbon type: (c) G and (d) CNT series. Typical Raman spectra of different carbon samples: (e) G and (f) CNT series.
FT-IR spectra of (a) G and (b) CNT series samples. UV absorption spectra of (c) G and (d) CNT series samples.

(a, c) Cathodic linear potential scan for determining the conduction band (CB) (b, d) anodic linear potential scan for determining the valence band (VB), and (e, f) schematic energy level diagrams of different specimens in comparison with the potentials for water reduction and oxidation.
Typical PL spectra of different graphene samples: (a) G and (b) CNT series. (c) Photographs of aqueous dispersions of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Optimization of graphene quantum dots by chemical exfoliation from graphite powders and carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

School of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work adopts one- and two-step modified Hummers' method to chemically exfoliate graphene nanosheets (GNs) from graphite flake powders and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). The protocol for making the GN products involves one- and two-step exfoliation using KMnO4 oxidation agent. Experimental results reveal that the two-step exfoliation induce high efficiency of chemical oxidation, especially for the synthesis of GNs from CNT precursor. The thermal reduction enables the removal of surface functionalities, thus narrowing the band gap of GN-based electrodes, e.g., from ∼2.12 eV to ∼1.60 eV. The two series of graphene samples, prepared from graphite flake and CNTs, are chemically cut into nanoscale and serve as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The photoluminescence (PL) excitation behavior is strongly affected by oxidation level and band gap of GQD products. After the thermal reduction, the PL peak shifts to lower wavelength region (i.e., 427–451 nm), offering a blue-shift behavior. The blue-shift behavior primarily originated from surface functionalization, particle size and shape of GQDs. Without any thermal reduction, the GQD suspensions exhibit an improved PL intensity, attributed to fast carrier‒carrier scattering dominates over the electron‒phonon scattering onto the basal plane of graphene sheets due to their high oxidation level and large band gap. As a result, this study offers an efficient way to tune the band gap of GQDs, favoring the development of optoelectronic and biological applications using graphene-based materials in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: The design and fabrication of nanostructured materials with extraordinary properties by using advanced methods has emerged as an alluring prospect for nanoscience and nanotechnology [1]. Recently, graphene oxide (GO) and graphene nanosheets (GNs) have received considerable attentions due to a variety of applications such as heat sinks [2–4], catalyst supports [5,6], Li-ion batteries [7–9], supercapacitors [10], solar cells [11,12], and transparent conducting films [13]. It is generally recognized that GO sheets consist of one atomic single or few-layer sheets of graphite decorated with oxygenated functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups) at edges or on basal planes, forming a hybrid structure comprising the mixture of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [14,15]. The oxygenated groups can be removed from the GO sheets by using thermal reduction in H2 and chemical reduction under microwave irradiation, resulting in reduced GO or GN products. Due to their efficiency and simplicity, Hummers' and modified Hummers' method have been considered as one of the most popular techniques to synthesize GO sheets [16–18]. The common approach to graphite exfoliation is the use of strong oxidizing agents to prepare GO sheets. Based on previous experience, micro-sized graphite powders are chemically oxidized with assistance of KMnO4 and NaNO3 in concentrated H2SO4 [18]. Several pioneering studies have reported one feasible preparation of GO-based samples from one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by treatment with KMnO4 or NaNO3 in H2SO4 [19,20]. The research achievements direct us at the synthesis of GO sheets from graphite flake and CNTs through one- and two-step chemical exfoliation.

More recently, significant advancements have been made in the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibiting marvelous properties with quantum confinement and edge effects [21,22]. Basically, the GQDs are of new zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots, which display strong photoluminescence (PL) intensity at its specific wavelength. Previous studies has confirmed that the possibility of tailoring the band gap of PL emission of GQDs, highly influenced by their size, morphology and surface functionalization [23–26]. However, few reports focus on the PL emission from GQDs prepared from graphite powders and CNTs through one- and two-step chemical exfoliation route. After the exfoliation of GO sheets from graphite, several approaches to downsize the GO sheets and GNs into GQDs have been developed using ultrasonic bath in H2O2 and H2SO4/HNO3 chemical etching. The present work aims at (i) the synthesis of high-quality GO sheets from graphite and CNTs using modified Hummers' method and (ii) the preparation of GQDs from the graphene-based materials through the aid of H2O2 in ultrasonic bath. The band gap and PL emission from as-prepared GQDs is systematically investigated. The present work would shed some lights on the feasibility for the blue PL emission from the GQDs, prepared by the modified Hummers' method using different carbon precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene by the modified Hummers' method
ce:section.ce:para:
The one- and two-step Hummers' method for preparing GNs from graphite flake and CNTs was briefly illustrated as Figs. 1 and 2
, respectively. As to the preparation of GNs from graphite flake, the one-step Hummers' method consisted of four steps including (i) implantation of HSO4 − and SO4 2− ions, (ii) the 1st replacement by MnO3 +, (iii) formation of oxygen functionalities on GO sheets, and (iv) thermal reduction of GNs from GO sheets. First, graphite flake (purity: 99.5%, approximately 3 μm in particle size) employed here was frequently used as conducting agent for energy storage devices such as Li-ion battery and supercapacitor. We placed 5 g graphite flake into 115 mL concentrated H2SO4 and then well mixed them in an ice bath for 1.5 h, i.e., step (i). After that, 15 g KMnO4 was carefully added into the slurry within 1 h while keeping the temperature less than 5 °C. The slurry was slowly stirred by a magnetic bar for 1 h and then heated to 35 °C, i.e., step (ii). Next, 200 mL distilled water was poured into the slurry. The temperature of the slurry was raised to 95 °C and maintained at this reaction temperature for 0.5 h. Afterward, the chemical oxidation was terminated by adding 500 mL H2O2 solution, i.e., step (iii), ensuring the implantation of oxygen functionalities (e.g., carbonyl and carboxyl groups) into the edges and basal planes of GO sheets. One thermal reduction process to reduce GNs from GO sheets was carried out at 400 °C under 5 vol% H2 atmosphere in a horizontal furnace, i.e., step (iv). The same procedure has also performed for making GO and GN products from the CNT precursor, as depicted in Fig. 2. The CNTs were grown by a catalytic chemical vapor deposition technique, using Ni-based metallic catalyst and C2H4 carbon source. The as-grown CNTs are of multi-walled type, having an average diameter of 50 nm and a length of several micrometers.

As for the two-step Hummers' method, the 2nd replacement of MnO3 + (i.e., step (ii’)) was carried out, leading to higher oxidation level on the carbon precursors, i.e., graphite flake powders and CNTs. To clarify the influence of chemical oxidation step on the oxidation level of the produced GO powders, 15 g KMnO4 was divided into 1st and 2nd replacement of MnO3 + for 1 h stirring process. Thus, both one- and two-step Hummers' method were based on the same basis of chemical oxidizing agent. Accordingly, the pathway for the two-step method involved step (i), (ii), (ii’), (iii), and (iv). The as-prepared samples were designated to two series: GO-G1, GO-G2, GN-G1, and GN-G2 (precursor: graphite flake, 1 and 2: one- and two-step chemical exfoliation) and GO-CNT1, GO-CNT2, GN-CNT1, and GN-CNT2 (precursor: CNT, 1 and 2: one- and two-step chemical exfoliation).

Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; JEOL JSM-6701F) and transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100) were adopted to inspect the micro-structural graphene samples. We employed an X-ray diffraction (XRD; Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000) spectroscope, equipped with Cu‒Kα radiation emitter, to characterize the crystallinity of graphene materials. The crystalline structures of GN and GO samples were also analyzed by using Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR; Nicolet Avatar 360 FTIR) spectroscopy was used to identify surface functional groups on graphene samples. For each sample, 32 scans in the spectral ranges were recorded with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The conduction and valence band edges of different graphene-based electrodes were examined using three-electrode electrochemical configuration system. A slurry-coating technique was used to prepare carbon coating onto stainless steel foil for the electrochemical analysis. First, all graphene samples were well mixed with a binder (poly-vinylidenefluoride) with the weight ratio of 80:20 in N-methyl pyrrolidinone (NMP) solvent to form the carbon slurry. Second, the graphene slurries were well blended with a three-dimensional mixer by using zirconia balls for 5 min. The as-prepared slurries were carefully pasted on the foil substrates (thickness: 20 μm) with a doctor blade, followed by evaporating the solvent (i.e., NMP) with a blow dryer. The as-prepared graphene layers with an area of 2 × 5 cm2 were then dried at 120 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. Finally, the electrode sheets were carefully pressed under a pressure of approximately 200 kg cm−2 with a compressive ratio of 40%. Prior to the electrochemical test, each electrode was cut into an area of 2 × 1 cm2. In the three-electrode system, Ag/AgCl and Pt wire were employed as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The electrolyte used in this work was 0.5 M sulfuric acid. The measurements were conducted by scanning the electrode potential from 0.4 to −1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a sweep rate of 5 mV/s at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and PL property of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To reduce the size of graphene products, both the GO sheets and GNs (0.05 g) were sonicated in 150 mL of H2O2 solution for 2 h. Afterward, the cutting process was conducting by refluxing the graphene slurries at 95 °C for 6 h. The suspensions were then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the solid precipitates were carefully collected after the removal of acid. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra of graphene suspensions (weight ratio: 0.05 mg GO and GN/1 mL distilled water) in quartz cell were analyzed and collected using an UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technology Cary 60) with a wavelength scan rate of 60 nm/min. Herein the distilled water served as blank. For PL characterization, the emission and excitation spectra of GQDs solutions recorded using an optic spectrometer (FLS920P Edinburgh Analytical Instrument), equipped with Xe lamp as an excitation source. The wavelength of excitation was set at 350 nm. The absolute quantum yields for all GQD samples were evaluated using an integrating sphere (i.e., barium sulfate) with a diameter of 150 mm. Herein all GQDs were diluted with distilled water to avoid re-absorption and other interferences. The PL measurements were also carried out at ambient temperature.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) present the FE-SEM images of pristine graphite flake and CNT sample, respectively. The images clearly show that the graphite flake displays of sheet-staked morphology, whereas the CNTs have a coiled tubular shape, ranged from 30 to 60 nm in outer diameter. The influence of chemically exfoliation number on the quality of GN products through the one- and two-step Hummers' procedure is further investigated by HR-TEM analysis (see Fig. 3(c)-(f)). The HR-TEM micrographs confirm that the one-step Hummers' procedure is ineffective to exfoliate both graphite flake and CNTs. It can be seen that both the graphite flake and CNTs basically maintain their morphologies. In the words, the GN-G1 sample still contains some multi-layer, whereas the GN-CNT1 sample possesses a tubular structure after the one-step chemical exfoliation process. After the two-step procedure, the GN-G2 sample shows a few-layer stacking structure, resulting from the fully chemical exfoliation. As to the GN-CNT2 sample, there are no tubular structures can be viewed, revealing that the oxidized tubular structure has been partially unzipped through the two-step exfoliation route.

The exfoliation efficiency of graphene samples was evaluated by using XRD and Raman spectroscopy. Typical XRD patterns of two series of graphene samples are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). It is generally recognized that the (002) diffraction peaks appear at approximately 26.2–26.4° for pristine G and CNT precursors, i.e., d (002)-interlayer spacing distance: 3.36 Å (G) and 3.41 Å (CNT). The one- and two-step exfoliations effectively induce the increased d (002) values to 3.89 and 5.90 Å for GO-G1 and GO-G2 samples, respectively. This reflects an obvious increase in the interlayer distance between each graphene layer, mainly originated from an oxidization-induced expansion from the presence of residual oxygen-containing functional groups or other structural defects [27]. As depicted in Fig. 4(c) and (d), it is worth noting that the increase in the d (002) values for CNT-series sample seems to be minor, e.g., d (002) values for GO-CNT1 and GO-CNT2: 3.42 and 3.46 Å. This can be presumably due to the difference between the morphology of CNT and graphite flake. Each single 1D CNT possesses both ends, which allow the attack of chemical oxidant (e.g., HSO4− and SO4 2− ions) on the opened ends. Thus, the tubular structure in the CNTs would cause the difficulty of implantation of oxygen functionalities during the chemical oxidation process. In contrast, the chemical oxidation easily takes place on the edges of microsized graphite powder, leading to a facile oxidation followed by an efficient exfoliation. After the thermal reduction in H2-containing atmosphere, the diffraction (002) peaks become broader and weaker, revealing the formation of GNs [28].

As shown in Fig. 4(e) and (f), typical Raman spectra show two main peaks centered at 1350 and 1580 cm−1, assigned to the D and G bands, respectively [29,30]. It is generally recognized that the intensity ratio of D to G band (i.e., I D/I G) is considered as a crucial factor in determining the graphitization degree of carbons, in which the D and G band represents the defects or structural disorder and the stacking of the graphite hexagon network plane. Herein the I D/I G ratios for two series of carbon samples are depicted in Fig. 4(e) and (f). Before the chemical exfoliation route, both I D/I G ratios of pristine G and CNT samples are less than 0.5, whereas the other I D/I G ratios increase up to 1.2–1.5 after the one- and two-step exfoliation route. This result implies an appearance of significant changes in the crystallinity. This increased I D/I G ratio can be attributed to the fact that the presence of lattice distortion (e.g., sp3/sp2 ratio) and surface functionalities (e.g., C‒OOH and C=O groups) that occurs in the stacking order of graphite lattice due to the chemical exfoliation approach.

FT-IR spectroscopy was adopted to analyze the distribution of functional groups on different graphene samples, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). The FT-IR spectra reflect that the presence of C=O and C‒O groups, centered at 1720 and 1080 cm−1, respectively [15]. It is worth noting that the intensity of transmittance band show an increase with the number of chemical exfoliation. This result reveals that the two-step chemical exfoliation route efficiently facilitates the oxidation level of graphene samples, raising the efficiency of graphene formation, confirmed by HR-TEM observation and XRD analysis. The UV–vis spectra of the resultant graphene suspensions shows an absorbance band in the 200–232 nm range, attributed from the characteristics π→π* electron transitions in the poly-aromatic system (i.e., C=C bond) of graphene samples [17,18,31]. The other band at > 300 nm can be assigned to the existence of n→π* transition of C=O bonds [17,18]. The overall features of the spectra and their absorbance peaks look similar for all graphene suspensions. It can be seen that the intensity of C=O absorbance band is significantly reduced after the thermal reduction, whereas the intensity is greatly enhanced by the two-step chemical exfoliation. Accordingly, the two-step exfoliation route enables the more oxygen functionalities attached to the graphene sheets than the one-step one. As expected, the thermal reduction is an effective way to strip and remove oxygen functionalities from the GO sheets. On the basis, the above result is well correlated with the previous FT-IR analysis.

One linear potential scan to analyze the conduction band edge and valence band edge positions of different graphene-based electrodes [32,33]. As illustrated in Fig. 6(a)-(d) , the abrupt cathodic and anodic currents of all electrodes were performed from the scans from 0.2 to −0.8 V and from 0.8 to 2.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. The conduction and valence band edges for each electrode can, thus, be determined from the sweep scans. Data from the linear scan profiles, Fig. 6(e) and (f) illustrate the energy level diagrams of graphene-based electrodes within the potential range, corresponding to water reduction and oxidation. The diagram evidences that the GN series sample shows smaller band gap (i.e., 1.59–1.91 eV), as compared to the GO series (i.e., 2.00–2.12 eV). This result indicates that the removal of oxygen functionalities is capable of narrowing the band gap of graphene samples. The narrow band gap significantly reduces the energy barrier of π electron conduction on the network of sp2-bonded carbon, as reported in our pervious study [34]. Additionally, we observe that both the GN-G1 and GN-G2 samples exhibit the narrower band gap, as compared to GN-CNT1 and GN-CNT2 ones. As a result, this is presumably due to the better accessibility of π electron conduction on 2D conductive network than that on 1D graphite-like structure.

Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the PL spectra of various GQD suspensions excited at wavelength of 340 nm, using a Xe lamp from the fluorescence spectrometer. To rule out the influence of particle size, the GQD samples are found to have the similar range from 1 to 3 nm, observed by atomic force microscopy (see Supporting Material). Basically, the PL spectra display a broad emission peak at different wavelengths, e.g., GO-G2 at 469 nm and GN-G2 at 427 nm. After the thermal reduction, the PL peak shifts from high wavelength region (i.e., 468–497 nm) to low wavelength region (i.e., 427–451 nm), inducing a blue-shift behavior. The blue-shift behavior primarily originates from the surface functionalization and the effect of particle size and shape [23,26]. Additionally, the PL intensity is dramatically reduced after the stripping of oxygen functionalities by the thermal reduction. It is of interest that the GO-G2 (100%) and GO-CNT2 (90%) suspensions offer the highest PL intensity among the GQD samples. For instance, the PL intensity of GO-G2 sample is three times higher than that of GN-G2. The PL intensity from the GO-CNT2 sample is 2.3 times greater than that from the GN-CNT2. The improved PL intensity could be ascribed to the difference in the band gaps of each graphene sample, related to carrier‒carrier and electron‒phonon (e‒h) scattering. Since the GO sheets prepared by the two-step exfoliation method possess high oxidation level and large band gap, this would facilitate that fast carrier‒carrier scattering dominates over the e‒h scattering onto the basal plane of graphene sheets. This phenomenon imparts one direct recombination of excited e‒h pairs, thus, producing high-intensity PL before thermalization of the carrier within the lattice [35–37].

Moreover, the GO-G2 suspension is found to have higher PL intensity than the GO-CNT2. This mainly originates from one reason that the GN-G2 sample provide 2D dielectric screening network, inducing high-density non-equilibrium carriers responsible for the faster e‒h recombination [26], as compared to 1D pathway on the CNT-based graphene samples (i.e., the GO-CNT2 sample). The photographs of various graphene suspensions, using alcohol as solvent and 340 nm lamp as excitation source, are taken and illustrated in Fig. 7(c). The GO-G2 and GO-CNT2 suspensions exhibit a bluish-green color, while the GN-G2 and GN-CNT2 samples display a deep-blue color. This observation is in accordance with the PL excitation spectra. On the basis of experimental results, the present work provide one straightforward route to synthesize high-intensity GQDs with various optical band gaps, based on the exfoliation number and the selection of carbon precursor with different morphologies. Since the chemical exfoliation route is efficient and simple, the GQDs can serve as a promising candidate for optoelectronic and biological applications.

We have experimentally synthesized graphene products from graphite flake and CNTs using one- and two-step modified Hummers' method. The protocol for running the chemical oxidation of carbon precursors involved one- and two-step exfoliation using KMnO4 oxidation agent. Experimental results revealed that the two-step exfoliation imparts high efficiency of chemical oxidation, especially for the formation of GNs from CNT precursor. The two series of graphene products, prepared from graphite flake powders and CNTs, showed different oxidation levels and band gaps, resulting in various PL excitation performances. The thermal reduction enabled the removal of surface functionalities, thereby narrowing the band gap of graphene products, e.g., GO-G2 (2.12 eV) and GN-G2 (1.60 eV). After the thermal reduction, the PL peak shifted to lower wavelength region (i.e., 427–451 nm), showing a blue-shift behavior. The blue-shift behavior primarily originated from the surface functionalization and the effect of particle size and shape. The GO-based GQD suspensions displayed an improved PL intensity, attributed to fast carrier‒carrier scattering dominates over the e‒h scattering onto the basal plane of graphene sheets due to their high oxidation level and large band gap. Accordingly, this work offered one efficient way to tune the band gap of GQDs, beneficial for the future development of optoelectronic and biological applications using graphene-based materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan, R.O.C.) under the contracts MOST 105-2628-E-155-002-MY3, MOST 105-2221-E-155-014-MY3 and MOST 105-2622-E-155-011-CC2.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2018.05.016.
